items.fix: Assert an independent invariant, not 'output == this thing I just computed via the same code'.
items.why: If the test recomputes the implementation, it'll always agree — even when both are wrong.
items.example:
```python
# BAD: re-implements chunking inside the assert
assert chunk_text(t) == [t[i:i+20] for i in range(0, len(t), 20)]

# GOOD: independent invariant
assert ''.join(chunk_text(t)) == t
```

items.why: Floats produced by different operation orders can differ in the last bit; equality tests will be flaky.

items.mistake: Overly loose strategies that find irrelevant inputs.
items.fix: Constrain strategies to the inputs your function actually has to handle.
items.why: `st.text()` includes surrogate pairs and NULs; if your tokenizer doesn't have to handle those, narrow the strategy.

items.mistake: Forgetting to pin known bug inputs with `@example`.
items.fix: Every bug PBT finds should be added back as `@example(...)` so it's tested deterministically every run.
items.why: Hypothesis randomness means the same bug might or might not be re-discovered later — pin it.

items.mistake: Using PBT for things with random / non-deterministic outputs (LLM responses).
items.fix: PBT works best on pure functions; for LLM behaviour, use evals on a fixed dataset, not Hypothesis.
items.why: If the output isn't a deterministic function of input, you can't define a falsifiable property.
items.example: Test the prompt *assembly* function with PBT; test the LLM *behaviour* with an eval suite.
